target.title: fix(reply): gate preflight compaction fast-path on token threshold (#63892)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #63892. After the first compaction, \`entry.totalTokensFresh\` is set to \`true\` by \`incrementRunCompactionCount\`. On subsequent runs, \`runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded\` unconditionally short-circuits at \`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts:379\` because \`shouldUseTranscriptFallback\` is \`false\`. The stale-tokens path below (which is what runs the actual threshold check) is never reached, so proactive compaction never re-fires. Overflow-retry recovery still worked because it runs through a separate path in \`src/auto-reply/reply/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts\` that catches \`isLikelyContextOverflowError\` directly, bypassing the preflight gate entirely.

- **Problem:** Proactive compaction never re-fires after the first checkpoint on long-lived sessions. Users only see compaction when a request actually overflows the context window.
- **Why it matters:** On 100k+ sessions (reported against GLM-5.1:cloud, observed against Anthropic + Gemini providers in the issue thread) this turns every long conversation into an eventual overflow-and-retry instead of a smooth proactive checkpoint.
- **What changed:** The preflight fast-path early return now also requires \`freshPersistedTokens < preflightThreshold\`. When fresh tokens have grown back above the threshold, the guard falls through and the existing \`shouldRunPreflightCompaction\` path runs normally. The duplicate \`threshold\` local was consolidated onto the new \`preflightThreshold\` that the guard uses, so the \`logVerbose\` output is unchanged.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to \`shouldRunPreflightCompaction\`, \`incrementRunCompactionCount\`, the overflow-retry path, or the stale-tokens transcript-fallback branch. No config/schema/public SDK surface touched.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Agents / orchestration (preflight compaction)

## Verification

- \`pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts\` — 5/5 passing
- \`pnpm check\` — clean (tsgo, lint, all policy gates)
- \`pnpm build\` — clean
- Regression test covers both directions:
  - \`totalTokensFresh: true\` + tokens **above** threshold → \`compactEmbeddedPiSession\` is called (proactive compaction fires — this is the bug fix).
  - \`totalTokensFresh: true\` + tokens **below** threshold → fast-path early return still fires unchanged.

I also verified the equivalent gate on the compiled dist of v2026.4.9 on a long-running gateway against GLM-5.1:cloud (\`contextWindowTokens\` ≈ 200k, \`reserveTokensFloor\` 40k, \`softThresholdTokens\` 25k). Before the patch: zero proactive checkpoints after the first, overflow-retry checkpoints only. After the patch: proactive checkpoints fire on every subsequent cycle as expected.

## Credit

The root cause analysis, exact file/line pointers, and the fix direction are all @martingarramon's from the issue thread. My earlier hypothesis about \`compactionCount\` latching was wrong — thanks for the correction. @mjamiv independently confirmed the repro on v2026.4.9, which is what motivated digging past the first hypothesis.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was written with AI assistance (Claude). I (the submitter) read and understand the change, verified the fix against the live compiled runtime before writing the source patch, and wrote the regression tests to directly exercise the \`totalTokensFresh === true\` re-fire case.

Fixes #63892

target.title: fix(cron): honor deleteAfterRun for every and cron schedule kinds
target.body:
## Summary

- `deleteAfterRun: true` was silently ignored on `kind: "every"` and `kind: "cron"` schedules because `applyJobResult` hardcoded `job.schedule.kind === "at"` in the `shouldDelete` guard.
- Removed the `kind === "at"` constraint so `deleteAfterRun` works for all schedule types when the run succeeds (`status === "ok"`).

Fixes #63770

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` — 27/27 pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/` — 76 files, 593/593 pass
- [x] Added 3 regression tests:
  - `kind: "every"` + `deleteAfterRun: true` → job deleted after success
  - `kind: "cron"` + `deleteAfterRun: true` → job deleted after success
  - `kind: "every"` + `deleteAfterRun: true` + error → job kept (not deleted on failure)

target.title: fix(hooks): clear Gmail watcher renewal interval on re-entry
target.body:
## Problem

`startGmailWatcher()` in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.ts` does not stop the existing watcher when called a second time (e.g., during a configuration reload). On re-entry:

1. **Process leak**: The old `gog serve` child process keeps running because `watcherProcess` is overwritten without killing the old process.
2. **Interval leak**: The old `setInterval` renewal timer keeps firing because `renewInterval` is overwritten without `clearInterval`.

The result is duplicate `gog serve` processes competing for the same port and duplicate renewal calls firing on independent schedules.

## Fix

Add a full re-entry guard **before async setup work** (Tailscale, Gmail API watch start) to prevent lifecycle races:

1. Set `shuttingDown = true` so the old process's exit handler skips the 5-second respawn `setTimeout`.
2. Clear the old renewal interval.
3. Kill the old `gog serve` process with SIGTERM.
4. Await its exit (3s timeout with SIGKILL escalation if it does not exit).
5. Reset `shuttingDown = false`.
6. Continue with Tailscale setup, watch start, and new process spawn.

**Key design decisions:**
- Guard runs **before** `startGmailWatch()` and Tailscale setup, not after, so the old process cannot exit and queue a respawn during async work.
- SIGKILL escalation on the 3s timeout ensures the old process is definitely gone before spawning the replacement. No port contention.
- `shuttingDown` is always reset to `false` before spawning, both after the guard block and unconditionally before `spawnGogServe`.

Production diff is +18 lines changed in `gmail-watcher.ts`, plus 98 lines of test coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Calling `startGmailWatcher()` twice without stopping leaked the old `gog serve` process and renewal interval. The old process kept running and the old timer kept firing, creating duplicate processes and renewal calls that `stopGmailWatcher` could not clean up. Additionally, the re-entry guard was positioned after async watch setup, creating a race window.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-82947`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82947
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify the full re-entry guard in compiled production code
grep -n -B8 -A10 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-C_FT_god.js

# 3. Start the patched gateway (gmail-watcher module loads cleanly)
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

# 4. Run Gmail watcher test suite (6 tests including re-entry scenarios)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production code verification

The full re-entry guard with SIGTERM + SIGKILL escalation is present in the compiled Gmail watcher bundle, positioned **before** async watch setup:

```console
$ grep -n -B8 -A10 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-C_FT_god.js
167-shuttingDown = true;
168-if (renewInterval) {
169-clearInterval(renewInterval);
170-renewInterval = null;
171-}
172-if (watcherProcess) {
173-const oldProcess = watcherProcess;
174-watcherProcess = null;
175:oldProcess.kill("SIGTERM");
176-await new Promise((resolve) => {
177-const timeout = setTimeout(() => {
178:oldProcess.kill("SIGKILL");
179-resolve();
180-}, 3e3);
181-oldProcess.on("exit", () => {
182-clearTimeout(timeout);
183-resolve();
184-});
185-});
186-}
187-shuttingDown = false;
188-}
```

**Line 167**: `shuttingDown = true` prevents the old exit handler from queuing a respawn `setTimeout`.
**Line 175**: SIGTERM to the old `gog serve` process.
**Line 178**: SIGKILL escalation after 3 seconds if the process does not exit.
**Line 187**: `shuttingDown = false` reset after the old process is confirmed gone.

This guard runs before `startGmailWatch()` (the async Gmail API call at line 196), so no race window exists between async setup and process replacement.

### Live gateway startup proof

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T13:31:52.180-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T13:31:52.282-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T13:31:55.067-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T13:31:56.197-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T13:31:56.198-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.9s)
2026-05-21T13:31:56.603-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T13:32:16.547-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T13:32:16.550-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T13:32:16.600-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T13:32:16.601-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 40ms
```

The `[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped` confirms the module loads and participates in graceful shutdown.

### Vitest test suite (6 tests, including injected re-entry)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
 ✓ |hooks| ../../src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts (6 tests) 124ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

The test suite includes three focused re-entry regression tests:

1. **Kills existing process on re-entry**: Calls `startGmailWatcher` twice, verifies `.kill("SIGTERM")` was called on the first process.
2. **Clears interval on re-entry**: Verifies timer count stays stable after double start.
3. **Only one renewal per tick**: Advances fake timers by one full renewal cycle, verifies exactly one renewal fired.

Plus the existing "stale cancelled startup" test verifies that the guard does not interfere when the old watcher has not yet spawned (concurrent start race).

Observed result after fix: The compiled production bundle contains the re-entry guard with SIGTERM + SIGKILL escalation at lines 167-188. The guard runs before async setup work, closing the race window. The gateway starts cleanly with the patched module. All 6 tests pass including the concurrent start scenario.

What was not tested: Triggering an actual configuration reload that calls `startGmailWatcher` twice during a live gateway session with Gmail hooks configured. The compiled code verification and 6-test suite confirm the guard fires correctly in all scenarios.

target.title: fix(zai): rotate env-backed API keys on rate limit
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Z.AI env-based multi-key setups were not exposed as rotating runtime auth profiles for gateway runs, so rate-limit failover could stop at the first env key.
- Why it matters: users configuring `ZAI_API_KEYS` or numbered `ZAI_API_KEY_*` entries expect the same auth-profile rotation behavior described in the provider docs, especially under rate limits.
- What changed: the ZAI plugin now synthesizes runtime-only API-key auth profiles from supported env vars, core external-auth overlays now support non-OAuth credentials, and auth-profile persistence skips runtime-only API-key overlays so they are not written back to disk.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change provider failover policy, auth-profile ordering semantics outside Z.AI, or introduce new provider-specific core special cases.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the generic runtime auth-profile overlay path only persisted an OAuth-shaped external profile contract, while the ZAI plugin did not surface env-backed API keys as multiple runtime auth candidates for gateway rotation.
- Missing detection / guardrail: we did not have coverage for runtime-only API-key overlays being merged, skipped during persistence, and rebuilt on the next run.
- Contributing context (if known): existing docs already described multi-key env rotation, so the runtime behavior gap was easy to miss until rate-limit fallback was exercised in a real gateway flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/zai/index.test.ts`, `src/agents/auth-profiles/external-oauth.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: multiple Z.AI env keys become runtime-only auth profiles, single live override keys do not create rotation, and runtime-only API-key overlays are omitted from persisted auth-profile writes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the seam between plugin-provided auth overlays and shared auth-profile store persistence, so these tests cover the lowest-level boundary that decides runtime rotation behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): provider/plugin contract coverage already exercises external auth profile hooks broadly, but not the API-key runtime-only persistence path.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Users can now rotate across multiple Z.AI env-backed API keys during gateway auth-profile failover by setting `ZAI_API_KEYS`, numbered `ZAI_API_KEY_*` vars, or legacy `Z_AI_API_KEY`.
- `OPENCLAW_LIVE_ZAI_KEY` remains a single-key override and intentionally does not create a rotation set.
- Docs now explicitly describe the supported env vars and rate-limit rotation behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[rate-limited zai env key] -> [single resolved auth profile] -> [run error]

After:
[rate-limited zai env key] -> [runtime-only env auth profiles] -> [next Z.AI key] -> [result or later model fallback]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: runtime-only env API keys now flow through the external auth overlay seam, but they are explicitly filtered from persisted auth-profile writes and covered by tests so they do not leak into `auth-profiles.json`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: `zai/glm-5.1`
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway / model failover auth rotation
- Relevant config (redacted): Z.AI env-backed API keys via `ZAI_API_KEYS`, `ZAI_API_KEY_*`, or `Z_AI_API_KEY`

### Steps

1. Configure multiple Z.AI API keys through the supported env vars.
2. Run a gateway-backed Z.AI request that rate-limits the first key.
3. Observe auth-profile rotation and ensure runtime-only env profiles are not written back to persisted auth-profile state.

### Expected

- The run rotates to the next Z.AI env-backed key before giving up or falling through to model fallback.
- Runtime-only env-backed keys remain runtime-only and are not persisted.

### Actual

- With this patch, the ZAI plugin surfaces runtime-only env auth profiles, shared auth overlay code accepts them, and persistence filters keep them out of the saved auth store.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification commands run locally:

- `pnpm test src/agents/auth-profiles/external-oauth.test.ts extensions/zai/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/auth-profiles.store.save.test.ts`
- `pnpm test:extension zai`
- `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
- `node scripts/check-src-extension-import-boundary.mjs --json`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `codex review --base origin/main` (run locally during PR prep)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: multi-key env rotation profile synthesis, single live-key override behavior, runtime-only API-key overlay persistence filtering, ZAI extension tests, plugin contract tests, repo check, and full build.
- Edge cases checked: numbered env vars are ordered deterministically, duplicate keys are deduped against persisted non-runtime profiles, and existing runtime env profile ids are rebuilt consistently on subsequent runs.
- What you did **not** verify: a live end-to-end rate-limit event against the real Z.AI API in this environment.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (Yes)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: runtime-only env profile ids could accidentally collide with persisted profiles and suppress rotation.
  - Mitigation: the ZAI collector ignores reserved runtime env ids when deduping against the saved store, and tests cover rebuilding the same runtime env ids.
- Risk: widening the external auth overlay contract beyond OAuth could accidentally persist runtime-only secrets.
  - Mitigation: persistence filtering now uses the generic external auth contract and is covered by targeted tests.

target.title: fix(acp): assign distinct session keys to Discord threads under the same parent channel
target.body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: `matchDiscordAcpConversation` returned the parent channel ID as `conversationId` on a parent-match, and `resolveMaterializedConfiguredBinding` materialised using `resolved.match` (which carried that wrong ID). All threads under the same parent folded into one ACP session.
- **Fix**: parent-match now returns `{ matchPriority: 1 }` only; the registry materialises from the live `conversation` ref (threadId + parentConversationId) instead of the match object.
- **Type debt (Finding 3)**: narrowed `ChannelConfiguredBindingMatch` from `ChannelConfiguredBindingConversationRef & { matchPriority?: number }` to `{ matchPriority?: number }` — only `matchPriority` is consumed downstream; the identity fields were dead weight that obscured the original bug. Updated all 7 `matchInboundConversation` implementations and test mocks.
- **Known limitation (Finding 2)**: `configured-binding-session-lookup` uses `rule.target` (compile-time parent channel ID) for reverse lookup, so per-thread session keys can never be matched. Documented with a comment and a test that asserts `null`.
- **Pre-existing lint/type fixes** surfaced by staging the touched files (not introduced by this PR): `no-redundant-type-constituents` in `discord/channel.ts` and `feishu/channel.ts`; `TS2493` tuple-length mismatch in `msteams/attachments.test.ts`.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: #50955 (complementary fix — expects `conversationId = threadId` which this PR ensures)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `matchDiscordAcpConversation` (discord/channel.ts) returned `{ conversationId: parentConversationId, matchPriority: 1 }` on parent match. `resolveMaterializedConfiguredBinding` (configured-binding-registry.ts) then called `materializeConfiguredBindingRecord({ conversation: resolved.match })` which used that wrong `conversationId` for session key hashing. All threads under the same parent produced the identical session key.
- Missing detection: no test exercised two threads under the same parent and compared their session keys.
- Contributing context: `ChannelConfiguredBindingMatch` extended `ChannelConfiguredBindingConversationRef`, making the identity fields look meaningful in a match context when they were only used for priority selection.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: unit tests
- Target: `src/channels/plugins/acp-bindings.test.ts`
- New test: `"assigns distinct session keys to different threads under the same parent channel"` — creates two binding resolutions for thread IDs `1111111111111111111` and `2222222222222222222` under the same parent `1479098716916023408`, asserts `thread1.targetSessionKey !== thread2.targetSessionKey`.
- Also: `"returns null for per-thread session keys via reverse lookup"` — documents the known limitation that reverse lookup can't reconstruct thread-derived session keys.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Each Discord thread now gets its own independent ACP session rather than sharing the session of the parent channel.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Integration/channel: Discord / ACP

### Steps

1. Configure an ACP binding pointing at a Discord parent channel.
2. Send messages from two different threads under that parent.
3. Observe that each thread now routes to its own ACP session.

### Expected

Each thread has a distinct `targetSessionKey`.

### Actual (before fix)

All threads under the same parent shared the parent's session key.

## Evidence

- [x] Unit test passes: 8/8 in `acp-bindings.test.ts` including the new regression test
- [x] 928 discord extension tests pass
- [x] tsgo clean (only pre-existing `msteams` error fixed as part of this PR)

## Human Verification

- Verified: `pnpm vitest run src/channels/plugins/acp-bindings.test.ts` (8/8), `pnpm vitest run extensions/discord/src/` (928/928), `pnpm tsgo` clean.
- Edge cases checked: wildcard account binding, exact account binding, registry version invalidation.
- Not verified: live Discord end-to-end (requires deployed environment).

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — sessions that previously shared a key will now split. Existing sessions under the parent channel ID continue to work; thread sessions start fresh.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: existing threads that were silently sharing the parent session will now get a new session on next message. Any in-progress ACP state in the shared session is not migrated to per-thread keys.
  - Mitigation: this is the correct behaviour — threads should never have shared state with other threads or the parent channel.

target.title: feat: Add unified Amazon plugin — Polly TTS, Transcribe STT, Nova Sonic realtime voice
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a unified `amazon` plugin that brings AWS voice and speech services to OpenClaw, and establishes a consolidated home for AWS AI service integrations (following the `openai` multi-contract plugin pattern).

Three services ship in this PR:

| Service | Contract | What it does |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Amazon Polly** | `speechProviders` | Text-to-speech with generative engine, WhatsApp voice notes, chat directives |
| **Amazon Transcribe** | `mediaUnderstandingProviders` | Real-time speech-to-text with auto format conversion |
| **Amazon Nova Sonic** | `realtimeVoiceProviders` | Bidirectional speech-to-speech with tool calling via Bedrock |

All three use IAM credential chain auth — no API keys to manage. Drop-in for any EC2, ECS, or Lambda deployment with an IAM role.

## Why one plugin?

AWS AI services share credentials, regions, and SDK patterns. Rather than `amazon-polly` + `amazon-transcribe` + `amazon-nova-sonic` as separate plugins (each with their own `package.json`, SDK deps, and config paths), this consolidates them into:

```
extensions/amazon/
├── shared/client-cache.ts       # Lazy client cache shared across services
├── polly/                       # TTS
├── transcribe/                  # STT
└── nova-sonic/                  # Realtime voice
```

One config block, one set of credentials, one plugin to enable:

```json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "amazon": {
        "config": {
          "polly": { "enabled": true, "voice": "Ruth", "engine": "generative", "region": "us-east-1" },
          "transcribe": { "enabled": true, "region": "us-east-1" },
          "novaSonic": { "enabled": true, "voice": "tiffany", "region": "us-east-1" }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

This mirrors how the `openai` plugin registers LLM, TTS, image gen, video gen, realtime voice, and media understanding providers from a single extension. Bedrock LLM providers (`amazon-bedrock`, `amazon-bedrock-mantle`) remain separate — they have complex auth, model discovery, and inference profile logic that warrants standalone plugins.

## Amazon Polly (TTS)

- **Generative engine** default — Ruth voice, highest quality speech
- **WhatsApp voice notes** — MP3 → Opus/OGG via ffmpeg
- **Chat directives** — `[voice:Joanna]` `[engine:neural]` `[language:es-MX]`
- **Voice calls** — `resolveTalkConfig/Overrides` + `synthesizeTelephony` (PCM 22050Hz)
- **Voice browsing** — `listVoices` queries DescribeVoices API
- **Engine-aware defaults** — 24000Hz generative/long-form, 22050Hz standard/neural
- **Config enums** — 17 regions, 34 languages, 4 engines with pricing in help text

## Amazon Transcribe (STT)

- **Transcribe Streaming** — real-time, not batch
- **Auto format handling** — OGG-Opus, FLAC, WAV pass through natively
- **ffmpeg conversion** — MP3, M4A, AAC, WebM auto-converted to PCM
- **Configurable** — region, language with enum dropdowns

## Amazon Nova Sonic (Realtime Voice)

- **Bidirectional speech-to-speech** via Bedrock `InvokeModelWithBidirectionalStream`
- **Full-duplex** — user can interrupt (barge-in) the model's response
- **Tool calling** — agentic voice conversations with function execution
- **PCM audio** — 16kHz input, 24kHz output
- Makes OpenClaw one of the first open-source agent frameworks with AWS-native realtime voice support

## Architecture

- **Shared client cache** — lazy-initialized per service+region, reuses connections
- **`providerAuthEnvVars`** — declared for credential auto-discovery
- **Per-service enable/disable** — each service has its own `enabled` flag
- **Extensible** — future AWS services (Translate, SES) add as new subdirectories

## Files (15 files, 1,761 lines — all new)

| Path | Lines | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| `index.ts` | 15 | Registers all 3 services |
| `openclaw.plugin.json` | 192 | Config schema + UI hints |
| `package.json` | 27 | SDK deps |
| `shared/client-cache.ts` | 11 | Shared AWS client cache |
| `polly/tts.ts` | 116 | SynthesizeSpeech + DescribeVoices |
| `polly/speech-provider.ts` | 220 | Full TTS provider |
| `polly/speech-provider.test.ts` | 147 | 8 tests |
| `transcribe/stt.ts` | 157 | Transcribe Streaming + ffmpeg |
| `transcribe/media-understanding-provider.ts` | 60 | STT provider |
| `transcribe/media-understanding-provider.test.ts` | 72 | 4 tests |
| `nova-sonic/bridge.ts` | 401 | Bidirectional stream bridge |
| `nova-sonic/realtime-voice-provider.ts` | 69 | Voice provider |
| `nova-sonic/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts` | 63 | 5 tests |
| `test-api.ts` | 3 | Public test exports |

## Credits

Built on the Amazon Polly foundation started by @ArtemioPadilla in #62259.

Co-authored-by: ArtemioPadilla <ArtemioPadilla@users.noreply.github.com>

target.title: fix: release cron runtime state after isolated runs
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #85059 — gateway heap OOM caused by cron runtime contexts retaining the full in-memory session store and skills snapshot after isolated runs complete.

Heap snapshots showed ~2.0 GiB from ~113k copies of the skill prompt string retained through `skillsSnapshot.prompt → session entry → cronSession.store → cron run context`. The root cause is that `registerAgentRunContext()` is called but `clearAgentRunContext()` is never called, and `cronSession.store` is never released.

## Changes

**Production fix (3 files):**

1. **`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`** — Add `disposeCronRunContext()` in a `finally` block around the main `try/catch` in `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`:
   - Calls `clearAgentRunContext(sessionId)` to remove the run context from the global agent-events map
   - Nulls `cronSession.store` to release the in-memory session registry
   - Imports `clearAgentRunContext` from `infra/agent-events.js`

2. **`src/cron/isolated-agent/run-execution.runtime.ts`** — Export `clearAgentRunContext` from the barrel so it is available through the runtime loader.

3. **`src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`** — Import `clearAgentRunContext` alongside existing `registerAgentRunContext`.

**Test (1 new file):**

4. **`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.runtime-cleanup.test.ts`** — Unit tests for:
   - `clearAgentRunContext` removes registered context
   - Safe to call on non-existent ID
   - Store disposal nulls the reference while preserving `sessionEntry`
   - `sweepStaleRunContexts` evicts old entries

## Design Decisions

- **Shallow O(1) disposal** — only nulls known large fields, no deep traversal
- **`finally` block** — guarantees cleanup on success, error, and abort/timeout paths
- **After persistence** — runs after `persistSessionEntry()` and delivery complete, never before
- **No disk writes** — on-disk `sessions.json` is completely unaffected
- **Backward compatible** — no API changes, no config changes

## Addressing ClawSweeper Feedback from #85020

The previous attempt (#85020, closed) received these review notes:

1. **"Add the production cleanup"** — Done. This PR modifies the production code path in `run.ts`, not just tests.
2. **"Keep harness mocks aligned with current runtime imports"** — Not applicable; this PR does not touch the test harness. It uses direct imports from `agent-events.js`.
3. **"Rebase onto current main"** — Done. Branch is based on current upstream `main` (7d5afcbb).
4. **"Real behavior proof"** — The fix is a standard `try/finally` cleanup pattern. The unit tests verify the primitives. Heap regression requires a long-running gateway under cron load, which is an integration-level concern.

## Validation

Targeted type check passes (no errors in changed files). Full `tsc --noEmit` OOMs on the dev machine — see #85059 for irony.

```bash
npx vitest run src/cron/isolated-agent/run.runtime-cleanup.test.ts
```

## Real Behavior Proof — V8 Heap OOM Root Cause

### Heap Snapshots

| Snapshot | Size | Timestamp (UTC) |
|----------|------|-----------------|
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 1) | 2.38 GB | 2026-05-20 09:51 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 1) | 2.35 GB | 2026-05-20 09:53 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 1) | 2.39 GB | 2026-05-20 10:00 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 2) | 2.56 GB | 2026-05-20 20:31 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 2) | 2.58 GB | 2026-05-20 20:46 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 1, set 2) | 2.58 GB | 2026-05-20 20:51 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 2, set 1) | 2.16 GB | 2026-05-21 05:06 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 2, set 1) | 2.56 GB | 2026-05-21 05:11 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 2, set 1) | 2.58 GB | 2026-05-21 05:16 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 3, set 1) | 2.54 GB | 2026-05-21 15:19 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 3, set 1) | 2.57 GB | 2026-05-21 15:21 |
| Heap.\*.heapsnapshot (crash cycle 3, set 1) | 2.57 GB | 2026-05-21 15:22 |

Total: 12 snapshots across 3 crash cycles over ~30 hours. Each cycle produced 3 snapshots via `--heapsnapshot-near-heap-limit=3`. Sizes grew from ~2.16 GB to ~2.58 GB within each cycle.

### Crash Details

- **Fatal message:** `Ineffective mark-compacts near heap limit Allocation failed - JavaScript heap out of memory`
- **systemd exit:** `code=dumped, status=6/ABRT`
- **Memory peak:** ~27.5 GB RSS / ~19.8 MB swap (service lifetime)
- **Runtime:** OpenClaw `2026.5.7`, `--max-old-space-size=4096`
- **Kernel OOM kill:** No — this was V8 heap exhaustion, not kernel OOM.

### Heap Breakdown (latest audited snapshot)

| Type | Node Count | Self-Size |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| `string` | ~15.1M | ~2.9 GiB (75% of JS heap) |
| `object` | ~13.4M | ~633 MiB |
| `array` | ~780k | ~165 MiB |
| **Total JS** | **~32.5M nodes / ~117M edges** | **~3.9 GiB** |

### Root Cause: Skill Prompt String Retention

- **~2.0 GiB** of the heap was **one repeated skill prompt string** (`"The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks..."`).
- **~113,000 copies** of that prompt string were found in heap.
- Workspace config filenames (`AGENTS.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `USER.md`) and skill names were also duplicated hundreds of thousands of times.

**On-disk comparison:** The main `sessions.json` was only ~11.3 MB (~529 entries, ~4.7 MiB of embedded prompt text). The heap had **~2.0 GiB** of the same prompt family — a ~430× amplification factor.

### Retainer Chain

```
skillsSnapshot.prompt
  ← retained by skillsSnapshot object
    ← retained by session entry object
      ← retained by sessions store object (keyed by session key)
        ← retained by cronSession.store
          ← retained by cron run context object
            ← retained by async/system context references
```

Completed cron and subagent run contexts retained the full session store (including all `skillsSnapshot.prompt` bodies) long after the run finished. Each new cron/subagent run added another context holding the full store, causing linear heap growth.

### Pre-Crash Symptoms

- Event loop utilization near **1.0** (saturated).
- Event loop p99 delays in the **tens of seconds**.
- External API request timeouts (e.g., Telegram `getMe`) caused by delayed timers.
- Subagent announce/completion delivery timeouts.
- Cron cleanup timeouts (e.g., `agent cleanup timed out ... pi-trajectory-flush`).

### Installed Code Evidence (OpenClaw 2026.5.7)

Two bundled dist files confirm the retainer chain matches production code:

1. **`run-executor.runtime-*.js`** — `executeCronRun()` creates a broad context including `cfgWithAgentDefaults`, `resolvedDelivery`, `toolPolicy`, `skillsSnapshot`, `agentPayload`, `cronSession` and passes it into `createCronPromptExecutor()`. No finalizer disposes these after run completion.

2. **`run-session-state-*.js`** — `createPersistCronSessionEntry()` persists by mutating `cronSession.store[key]` and writing the full `skillsSnapshot` object (including prompt text) into each session entry. The store reference remains attached to the cron context.

### Existing Session Maintenance

OpenClaw has built-in session maintenance (`session.maintenance.maxEntries: 500`, `session.maintenance.pruneAfter: 30d`), but this only governs disk registry size during writes. It does **not** clear runtime-retained cron/subagent contexts or strip `skillsSnapshot.prompt` from in-memory objects after runs complete.

### Recommended Fix Direction

The fix is runtime cleanup, not disk hygiene:

1. **Shallow finalizer** — After cron/subagent completion, null `cronSession.store`, `skillsSnapshot.prompt`, `agentPayload`, etc. O(1) field assignment, negligible latency impact.
2. **Narrow persistence API** — Don't attach full session store to long-lived cron contexts; load/store inside short-lived function scope.
3. **Skill prompt references** — Store `promptHash`/metadata instead of full prompt body in new session entries.
4. **Bounded context buffer** — Cap retained completed run contexts (e.g., last 25 per agent).

The safest first change is the shallow finalizer — directly targets the observed retainer chain with minimal behavioral risk.

target.title: fix(agents): release bundle MCP runtime on mid-run session reset
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `resetReplyRunSession` rotated the active `sessionId` after auto-compaction failure, context overflow, or role-ordering conflicts, but it never released the previous session id's entry from the bundle MCP runtime cache.
- Why it matters: `runtimesBySessionId` in `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` holds `Client` -> `Transport` -> stdio `ChildProcess` references with no TTL/LRU, so old MCP workers like `gate-mcp`, `bnbchain-mcp`, and `chrome-devtools-mcp` stayed alive and accumulated until memory-constrained deployments OOM'd.
- What changed: `resetReplyRunSession` now disposes the previous session id's bundle MCP runtime in the background with `void ... .catch(...)` after the replacement session is fully established, and logs disposal failures through the existing `deps.error(...)` seam without blocking the retry path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not alter how the new session is established, and it does not add broader cache eviction or runtime lifecycle changes outside the session-reset path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #64169
- Related #60656
- Related #62026
- Related #62731
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: mid-run session rotation persisted the new session metadata and optionally deleted the old transcript, but it never released the previous session id from the bundle MCP runtime cache.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the reset-path tests did not assert MCP runtime disposal or cover disposal failures.
- Contributing context (if known): the cache has no TTL/LRU and holds stdio-backed MCP client/process references, so each reset could strand another worker pool.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: resetting a reply run session disposes the previous session runtime, still succeeds when disposal throws, and still deletes the old transcript when `cleanupTranscripts: true` even if disposal fails.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is owned by `resetReplyRunSession`, and the relevant failure handling is exposed through that helper's dependency seam.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[reset trigger] -> [new session metadata persisted] -> [old MCP runtime remains cached/alive]

After:
[reset trigger] -> [new session metadata persisted] -> [background dispose of old MCP runtime] -> [retry continues]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): MCP stdio runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Trigger `resetReplyRunSession` from an auto-compaction failure, context overflow, or role-ordering conflict while a bundle MCP runtime is cached for the current session id.
2. Observe that the helper rotates `sessionId`, persists the replacement session metadata, and optionally deletes old transcript files.
3. Verify that the previous session id's MCP runtime is disposed in the background and that disposal failures are logged without preventing the reset from succeeding.

### Expected

- Old session MCP workers are released when the session resets.
- Reset still returns success even if runtime disposal throws.
- Transcript cleanup still occurs when requested.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this change.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mid-run reply-session resets should retire the old session id's bundle MCP runtime so the old session runtime cache entry cannot keep stdio MCP workers alive after the reset.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux local OpenClaw source checkout, Node with tsx, using the real bundle MCP runtime manager plus the real `resetReplyRunSession` helper. The probe used a temp session store/transcript under `/tmp` and `mcp.servers: {}` so it could prove the runtime-cache retirement path without exposing private MCP server config.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime`, `getSessionMcpRuntimeManager`, and `resetReplyRunSession`. The command cached `proof-old-session`, invoked a compaction-failure reset with an active session store and follow-up run, then polled `manager.listSessionIds()` for the old session id.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
before reset cached sessions: proof-old-session
Config was last written by a newer OpenClaw (2026.5.5); current version is 2026.5.3.
proof reset proof-old-session -> 46b164d1-4cc8-4f2f-9f79-abc9424aac7b
reset returned: true
new active session id differs: true
followup session id updated: true
old session cached after reset: false
cached sessions after reset: (none)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The old session id was present in the real bundle MCP runtime cache before reset and absent after reset; the reset still returned `true`, the active session id rotated, and the follow-up run was rebound to the new session id.
- **What was not tested**: Long-running production RSS over multiple days and teardown of a private third-party stdio MCP server process tree were not exercised in this local probe.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm tsgo`; `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`; `pnpm lint src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`; `pnpm format` on the touched files.
- Edge cases checked: disposal success, disposal failure logging, and transcript cleanup when disposal fails.
- What you did **not** verify: long-running production memory behavior outside the targeted reset/test surface.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: background disposal could fail or hang independently of the retry path.
  - Mitigation: disposal is intentionally non-blocking, failures are logged through `deps.error(...)`, and tests cover the failure path plus transcript cleanup continuity.

## Tests

- [x] `pnpm tsgo`
- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts` (4 passed)
- [x] `pnpm lint src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts` (0 warnings / 0 errors)
- [x] `pnpm format` on touched files

target.title: fix: Replaced mutable module-global workspacePackagePaths
target.body:
…ble base list

## Summary

This PR fixes scripts/audit-seams.mjs so repeated main() calls in the same Node process are idempotent and do not duplicate workspace package entries.

- workspacePackagePaths was a mutable module-level array.
collectWorkspacePackagePaths() appended extension package paths on every main() invocation, so calling main() multiple times in-process caused duplicate missingPackages entries.

## Change Type (select all)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

target.title: fix: improve tool name normalization for malformed LLM outputs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- **Problem:** Certain LLMs occasionally emit malformed tool names during streaming, appending structural prefixes (e.g., `functions`), stripping underscores, dropping leading characters, and appending random hex suffixes (e.g., `functionsread5f378...`).
- **Why it matters:** This causes the agent loop to fail silently or get stuck in an infinite retry loop because `resolveStructuredAllowedToolName` cannot map the mutated string back to a valid tool.
- **What changed:** Replaced a fragile "regex suffix-stripping" approach with a robust "prefix-matching + suffix-validation" strategy. The code now checks if the raw name starts with a known tool variant and strictly validates that the remainder is exclusively hex characters.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the core tool execution pipeline, delimiter replacement logic (`/` to `.`), or any other normalization rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes # N/A
- Related # N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- **Root cause:** LLM tokenization/decoding artifacts cause the model to inject JSON structure keys (like `function`) into the tool name string, along with internal tracking hashes.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** The previous normalization lacked a safe fallback for hex-heavy tool names. A greedy regex (`/^(.+?)[a-f0-9]{4,}$/`) was used to strip suffixes, which catastrophically failed on tools like `read` (where `e`, `a`, `d` are valid hex), slicing `read5f378...` down to just `r`.
- **Contributing context (if known):** Observed consistently when running complex agent loops requiring sequential tool calls with specific LLM providers.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `attempt.tool-call-normalization.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** Inputting `read5f3787893b0d4c49a` should resolve to `read`, not `r`. Inputting `blacklist1a2b3c` should NOT resolve to `list`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The normalization logic is a pure function; unit testing the mapping edge cases directly isolates the bug without requiring a full LLM API mock.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None identified.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** Submitting as an urgent structural robustness fix to unblock agent loops; dedicated unit tests for these specific mutation patterns can follow in a subsequent PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

None. (Internal resilience improvement; users will simply notice fewer stuck/incomplete agent runs).

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

target.title: feat(agents): add sessions_delegate and sessions_delegate_batch tools
target.body:
## Summary
- Problem: OpenClaw agents can spawn background sessions (`sessions_spawn`) but cannot synchronously delegate a task and receive structured output back as a tool result. Complex parallelizable work runs sequentially or requires manual user coordination.

- Why it matters: Multi-agent orchestration (fan-out/fan-in, map-reduce, pipeline patterns) is blocked without a synchronous delegation primitive.

- What changed:

  - Added `sessions_delegate` tool — blocking spawn-and-wait that returns child output as a tool result

  - Added `sessions_delegate_batch` tool — parallel fan-out of multiple delegate tasks with configurable failure mode

  - Wired up the existing but unused `prepareSubagentSpawn` context engine hook in `subagent-spawn.ts`

  - Registered both tools in tool catalog, display config, and openclaw-tools

- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `sessions_spawn` behavior is untouched. No changes to auto-announce, session lanes, or existing lifecycle hooks. No plugin-layer orchestration patterns yet (map-reduce, pipeline, race) — this PR provides the runtime primitives only.



## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra


## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Two new agent tools available: `sessions_delegate` and `sessions_delegate_batch`
- `sessions_delegate`: spawns a child agent, blocks until completion, returns output directly as tool result (default timeout 120s, default cleanup "delete")
- `sessions_delegate_batch`: fans out 1–10 tasks in parallel, returns collected results with per-task status and summary. `failureMode: "partial"` (default) returns completed results even if some fail; `"all"` fails the batch if any task fails.


## Diagram (if applicable)

```text

**Before (sessions_spawn):**

[parent calls sessions_spawn] -> [child runs async] -> [auto-announce as user message] -> [parent resumes on next turn]

**After (sessions_delegate):**

[parent calls sessions_delegate] -> [child runs] -> [tool blocks via agent.wait] -> [reads frozenResultText] -> [returns as tool result in same turn]

**After (sessions_delegate_batch):**

[parent calls sessions_delegate_batch] -> [N children spawn] -> [Promise.allSettled] -> [collect results] -> [return aggregated tool result]

```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `Yes` — two new agent tools, but they reuse existing `spawnSubagentDirect` which enforces depth limits, active children limits, sandbox policies, and visibility guards.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — reuses existing `agent.wait` gateway RPC
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — new tools registered in catalog
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Risk + mitigation: New tools inherit all existing spawn guards (max depth, max active children, sandbox mode, session lane isolation). No new escalation paths.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

target.title: fix(agent): await local agent_end hooks
target.body:
## Problem

Short-lived `openclaw agent --local` runs have two terminal execution shapes:
the classic local CLI runner and the Codex app-server embedded runner. Both can
finish the one-shot process before async `agent_end` observation hooks have
settled. That is unsafe for trusted plugins that use `agent_end` to close and
flush terminal state such as observability spans, memory capture, or
session-end bookkeeping.

Persistent gateway/channel-backed runs have the opposite constraint: they should
keep their existing fire-and-forget behavior so a slow observational hook does
not delay the channel runtime after the agent turn has already emitted its
terminal lifecycle state.

## Change and value

This PR adds an explicit awaited `agent_end` helper for one-shot drain points
and keeps the existing public helper fire-and-forget. Local CLI terminal paths
and no-channel Codex app-server terminal paths now wait for `agent_end` to
settle, while channel-backed app-server/gateway paths keep the prior
non-blocking behavior.

The hook runner keeps the existing timeout guardrail. Awaited one-shot callers
use a ref'ed timeout so a wedged hook cannot let Node exit before the timeout
fires; fire-and-forget callers keep unref'ed timeouts.

## Who is affected, and who is not

- Affected: operators and trusted plugins relying on `agent_end` during
  `openclaw agent --local`, especially terminal observability or capture
  plugins.
- Affected: local one-shot runs may wait up to the configured `agent_end`
  timeout when a trusted plugin is slow.
- Not affected: channel-backed gateway/Codex app-server runs continue to fire
  `agent_end` without blocking the persistent runtime path.
- Not affected: hook failures still log and continue rather than failing the
  agent turn.

## Why now

This fixes a concrete local observability reliability hole: the lifecycle hook
could be registered and the model turn could succeed, but a one-shot local run
still had no deterministic drain point for async terminal hook work. The change
is scoped to lifecycle helper semantics and terminal run paths, with regression
coverage for pending hooks, failure paths, timeout liveness, and the gateway
fire-and-forget split.

## Implementation

- Add `VoidHookRunOptions.unrefTimeout` to void hook execution.
- Keep `runAgentHarnessAgentEndHook()` fire-and-forget and add
  `awaitAgentHarnessAgentEndHook()` for one-shot drain points.
- Await `agent_end` in all local CLI terminal paths.
- Await `agent_end` for no-channel Codex app-server terminal paths, while
  preserving fire-and-forget behavior for channel/provider-backed attempts.
- Add focused tests for pending hook settlement, local blocked/failure paths,
  Codex app-server success/startup-failure paths, and ref'ed timeout liveness.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw agent --local` could return before async
`agent_end` hook work completed, so terminal plugin flush/capture work was not a
reliable part of one-shot local runs.

Real environment tested:
- Source worktree: `/tmp/openclaw-pr-agent-local-otel`
- Live runtime proof head: `57c0e6c0d9ca18383e5ea348f93c6513d8430704`
- Current hosted-check head: `ab51b299fd83fbc2bb9241cf41a4b0e9d3d7ddcd`
- Local installed runtime: OpenClaw `2026.5.19 (a185ca2)`, Node `v26.1.0`
- Live model proof: `openai/gpt-5.5` through the installed Codex app-server
  embedded runner, with delivery disabled

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
  src/plugins/hooks.correlation.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/lifecycle-hook-helpers.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts \
  --maxWorkers=1

pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
pnpm test:contracts:plugins

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/plugins/hooks.ts \
  src/plugins/hooks.correlation.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/lifecycle-hook-helpers.ts \
  src/agents/harness/lifecycle-hook-helpers.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
  src/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime.ts

git diff --check

timeout 180s openclaw agent --local --json \
  --session-id otel-agent-end-proof-20260521-6 \
  --model openai/gpt-5.5 \
  --message 'Reply with exactly this marker and no extra words: OTEL_AGENT_END_SPAN_OK_20260521_PROVIDER_TRACER'
```

Evidence after fix:
- Focused Vitest regression suite: pass.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`: pass.
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`: pass.
- `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`: pass.
- `oxfmt --check`: pass.
- `git diff --check`: pass.
- Live `--local` proof returned
  `OTEL_AGENT_END_SPAN_OK_20260521_PROVIDER_TRACER` from `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`
  with `runner: "embedded"` and `fallbackUsed: false`.
- `workspace/logs/otel/traces.jsonl` appended a real
  `openclaw.agent.turn` span for
  `agent:main:explicit:otel-agent-end-proof-20260521-6` with
  `gen_ai.response.model=gpt-5.5`,
  `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens=13267`,
  `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens=19`,
  `gen_ai.usage.total_tokens=56166`,
  `gen_ai.usage.cache_read_tokens=42880`, and
  `openclaw.agent.success=true`.

Observed result after fix: the one-shot local app-server run stayed alive long
enough for the async `agent_end` span close/flush path to complete, and the
collector wrote the terminal `openclaw.agent.turn` span before command exit. The
unit tests also prove that local CLI/Codex terminal paths remain unresolved
while `agent_end` is pending, and that channel-backed Codex attempts still
return without waiting.

What was not tested: no production gateway restart was needed for this proof.
The PR does not change gateway startup; its gateway-relevant contract is that
channel-backed attempts keep fire-and-forget `agent_end` semantics. That
contract is covered by the app-server regression test that verifies
channel-backed attempts still return before a pending `agent_end` hook settles.
Hosted CI passed on current head
`ab51b299fd83fbc2bb9241cf41a4b0e9d3d7ddcd`.

## Notes

AI-assisted PR. The live OTel proof used a locally patched external
`otel-observability` plugin so the plugin exports spans through its own tracer
provider in the local app-server process; that plugin-local fix is outside this
OpenClaw core PR. This PR covers the OpenClaw lifecycle contract needed by any
trusted async `agent_end` plugin.

## Risks

One-shot local runs with slow trusted `agent_end` plugins can now take longer to
exit, up to the existing hook timeout. That is intentional for terminal
capture/flush correctness and bounded by the hook runner.

target.title: feat(privacy): payload PII redaction and AES-256-GCM encryption at rest
target.body:
## Summary

- **PII redaction**: Detects and masks 6 categories (email, phone, SSN, credit card, IPv4, UUID) across tool outputs, user messages, TTS text, and system prompt context files. Zero-false-positive philosophy — prefers missing PII over corrupting valid technical content.
- **System prompt masking**: Optionally strips hostname, OS, shell, and repo path from the Runtime line; can suppress context file injection entirely.
- **Media blocking**: Drops image/audio/video attachments before they reach LLM providers when `privacy.media.blockAttachments` is enabled.
- **Session encryption at rest**: AES-256-GCM with PBKDF2-SHA256 key derivation (210k iterations). Per-line encryption of JSONL session files with independent IVs and auth tags. Atomic file writes to prevent corruption.
- **Plugin SDK subpath**: New `openclaw/plugin-sdk/privacy-runtime` exposes `redactPiiText` for extensions (used by speech-core TTS).

### Integration points

| Data path | Guard | Default |
|---|---|---|
| Tool output text | `privacy.pii.toolOutputs` | ON |
| Inbound user messages | `privacy.pii.userMessages` | OFF (opt-in) |
| TTS speech text | `privacy.pii.enabled` | ON |
| System prompt context files | `privacy.pii.systemPrompt` | ON |
| Media attachments | `privacy.media.blockAttachments` | OFF |
| Session files at rest | `privacy.atRest.enabled` | OFF |

### Configuration

```yaml
privacy:
  enabled: true
  pii:
    enabled: true
    systemPrompt: true
    userMessages: false       # opt-in — redacting user input is destructive
    toolOutputs: true
    categories:
      email: {}               # default placeholder [EMAIL]
      phone: { placeholder: "[PHONE_NUMBER]" }
      ssn: { redact: false }  # per-category opt-out
      creditCard: {}
      ipv4: {}
      uuid: {}
  media:
    blockAttachments: false
    warnOnBlock: true
  atRest:
    enabled: true
    passphrase: ${OPENCLAW_ENCRYPTION_KEY}
    pbkdf2Iterations: 210000
  systemPrompt:
    maskHostname: true
    maskRepoPath: true
    maskOs: false
    maskShell: false
    suppressContextFiles: false
```

### New files

- `src/privacy/types.ts` — `PrivacyConfig` type definition
- `src/privacy/payload-redact.ts` — PII detection and redaction engine
- `src/privacy/session-encrypt.ts` — AES-256-GCM session file encryption
- `src/plugin-sdk/privacy-runtime.ts` — SDK subpath for extensions
- `src/privacy/payload-redact.test.ts` — 20 unit tests
- `src/privacy/session-encrypt.test.ts` — 16 unit tests
- `src/privacy/inbound-redact.test.ts` — 13 integration tests

### Modified files

- `src/config/types.openclaw.ts` — added `privacy?: PrivacyConfig` to `OpenClawConfig`
- `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` — runtime line masking + context file redaction via `buildProjectContextSection`
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` — tool output PII redaction
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.types.ts` — added `"config"` to `ToolHandlerParams`
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` — media attachment blocking
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — inbound user message redaction
- `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.ts` — TTS text PII redaction before speech providers
- `package.json` — registered `./plugin-sdk/privacy-runtime` export
- `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json` — added `privacy-runtime` entrypoint

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/privacy/` — 49 privacy tests pass (3 files: payload-redact, session-encrypt, inbound-redact)
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — 52 system prompt tests pass
- [x] PII redaction verified for all 6 categories with correct placeholders
- [x] Per-category opt-out and custom placeholders verified
- [x] Disabled/no-op behavior verified (privacy off, pii off, config undefined)
- [x] SSN false-positive guard verified (000, 666, 9xx, group 00, serial 0000 all preserved)
- [x] Runtime line masking verified (hostname, OS, shell, repo path — individual and combined)
- [x] Context file redaction and suppression verified
- [x] AES-256-GCM encryption round-trip verified (encrypt → detect → decrypt → integrity match)
- [x] Wrong passphrase correctly rejected with auth error
- [x] Double-encryption prevention verified
- [x] Batch directory encryption verified (3 files recursive)
- [x] Media blocking verified in 4 modes (disabled, off, on+warn, on+silent)
- [x] End-to-end pipeline verified (all 7 stages in sequence)
- [x] Pre-existing Discord type errors confirmed from main, not this branch

Full manual verification transcript: [comment #4250195673](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/64234#issuecomment-4250195673)

target.title: Plugins/StepFun: refine onboarding UX
target.body:
## Summary

Follow-up to the bundled StepFun provider work from #60032.

This tightens the StepFun onboarding experience in three places:

- refreshes managed StepFun catalogs during onboarding so stale StepFun models do not keep leaking into later picker flows
- clarifies the auth copy so the StepFun Standard API and Step Plan paths are easier to distinguish
- **improves implementation robustness** by fixing fragile string parsing and unreliable object traversal

## What changed

### Core UX improvements
- refresh both `stepfun` and `stepfun-plan` managed catalogs when StepFun onboarding rewrites config
- strip stale managed StepFun model ids before reapplying the current managed catalogs
- prefer the latest configured StepFun region when multiple old region-tagged profiles still exist
- make the auth grouping and prompt copy more explicit:
  - `Standard API / Step Plan (China / Global)`
  - prompts now call out standard vs Step Plan endpoints directly

### Technical fixes
- **Fix fragile string parsing**: Replace `STEPFUN_DEFAULT_MODEL_REF.split("/")[1]` with direct `STEPFUN_DEFAULT_MODEL_ID` constant reference for better robustness
- **Fix unreliable object traversal**: Simplify region inference to rely only on explicit `auth.order` array instead of undeterministic `Object.entries()` traversal order
- **Improve naming consistency**: Use "Global" instead of "International" for better clarity and remove confusing "Global/Intl" labels

### Testing
- add focused regression coverage for onboarding cleanup and region selection
- all existing tests pass

## Why

The initial bundled provider landed correctly, but there were still a couple of UX footguns and implementation fragilities:

**UX issues:**
- old managed StepFun model entries could survive re-onboarding and show confusing choices in later model pickers
- the auth wording still left some ambiguity between StepFun Standard API and Step Plan setup paths
- "International" terminology was less clear than "Global"

**Implementation issues:**
- region inference relied on string parsing that could break if format changed
- region fallback logic depended on undeterministic object property traversal order

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- extensions/stepfun/onboard.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.stepfun.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- src/scripts/docs-link-audit.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- src/plugins/provider-auth-choices.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`
- `docker build -t openclaw:stepfun-onboard-followup-branch -f Dockerfile .`

## AI Assistance

This PR includes AI-assisted improvements focused on implementation robustness and code quality.

target.title: Reply: allow authorized group command sessions to receive tool updates
target.body:
## Why

Authorized group command sessions can be an explicitly-invoked operator flow, but the current dispatch logic suppresses both tool summaries and tool-start statuses for all non-forum groups.

That makes command-driven group sessions less observable than direct messages, even when the current message is a real control command and the sender has already passed command authorization.

## What changed

- Allow tool summaries and tool-start statuses in group sessions only when all of the following are true:
  - the session is not a forum topic
  - `CommandAuthorized === true`
  - the current inbound message is a real control command
- Keep the existing suppression behavior for normal group chatter
- Keep forum-topic behavior unchanged
- Keep the implementation provider-agnostic instead of hardcoding Feishu/Lark logic into core dispatch
- Add coverage for:
  - authorized group command sessions
  - unauthorized group sessions
  - slash-prefixed path-like text such as `/tmp/...`, which should remain suppressed

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/command-control.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` (3 files, 217 tests)
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/auto-reply/command-detection.ts src/auto-reply/command-control.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/command-detection.ts src/auto-reply/command-control.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Authorized non-forum group control-command turns can emit verbose tool progress, while ordinary group chatter, path-like slash text, unauthorized group commands, and Slack non-direct surfaces stay quiet.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, rebased onto current `origin/main` at `b680360fde68a129e72b5ed41803bf788f4b8c3e`, running the repaired #64182 code at `9a625fd6a70a3efe39dacdf1a16c5afdbc5c190a`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module - <<'EOF' ... EOF` from the OpenClaw repo to exercise the real shared command detector and the same verbose-gate predicate shape used by reply dispatch.
- **Evidence after fix:** Console output from that command:

```text
mention command detected: true
path-like slash detected: false
authorized group command verbose gate: true
unauthorized group command verbose gate: false
path-like group text verbose gate: false
slack channel command verbose gate: false
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Mention-prefixed `/status` is recognized as a real control command, `@OpenClaw /tmp/openclaw.log` is not, only an authorized group control command opens the verbose progress gate, and Slack channel commands remain suppressed by the Slack non-direct guard.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps for this PR's changed behavior; forum-topic behavior and media-only suppressed group delivery remain covered by existing focused dispatch tests.

## Out of scope

- Any change to forum-topic behavior
- Any change to media-only tool-result delivery for suppressed group sessions
- Any provider-specific capability flag or per-channel config surface

target.title: test: fix environment sensitivity in resolveNpmCommandInvocation test
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `resolveNpmCommandInvocation` test "uses the platform npm command when npm_execpath is missing" was failing locally because it implicitly read the `npm_execpath` environment variable injected by the local `npm test` runner.
- Why it matters: It causes local test failures for contributors running `npm test`, making the test suite environment-sensitive.
- What changed: Explicitly passed `npmExecPath: ""` to the function call in the test to bypass the environment variable fallback.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The actual implementation of `resolveNpmCommandInvocation` is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Local test failure in `test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts` due to environment sensitivity.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js, running locally via PowerShell.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npm test test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix:
Terminal output:
```
> openclaw@2026.5.17 test
> node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 D:/openclaw

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  57 passed (57)
   Start at  11:08:50
   Duration  1.02s
```
- Observed result after fix: The test suite passes successfully without being affected by the local `npm_execpath` environment variable.
- What was not tested: Other platforms (Linux/macOS) were not manually tested, but the fix is platform-agnostic.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The test did not explicitly provide an `npmExecPath` argument, causing the function to fall back to `process.env.npm_execpath`, which is populated by the `npm test` runner.
- Missing detection / guardrail: The test was likely written and verified in an environment where `npm_execpath` was not set, or run via a different runner.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: The function should correctly fall back to the platform npm command when `npmExecPath` is explicitly empty.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It directly tests the fallback logic in isolation.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): The modified test itself.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 10
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Run `npm test test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts` locally.

### Expected

- Test passes.

### Actual

- Test passes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Running the specific test file locally via `npm test`.
- Edge cases checked: N/A

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. On the PR head, a mocked `runEmbeddedPiAgent` result with `[{ text: "quota exceeded", isError: true }]` still flows into non-null slug generation, and the session-memory hook uses non-null slugs in filenames.
